agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature where customers can view their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements — Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, Operations demands no location sharing at all. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved by technical design alone; it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver or delivery person physically transporting a package from origin to destination."},{"term":"Real-time Location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates as reported by their device during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active Delivery","definition":"The period from when a courier picks up a package until it is delivered or the delivery attempt concludes."},{"term":"Customer","definition":"The end recipient of a package who has placed or is expecting an order."},{"term":"Location Sharing","definition":"The act of making courier positional data visible to another party (e.g., customer)."},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — a derived metric based on courier location, route, and traffic, distinct from raw GPS coordinates."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for a long period (e.g., stuck in traffic, on break) — exact coordinates make this visible and invite customer complaints or courier harassment.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the core conflict is resolved."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal stop) — exact GPS exposes this to the customer.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the core conflict is resolved."},{"scenario":"Courier is near destination but not arriving (e.g., looking for parking, access issues) — exact GPS creates anxiety and support calls.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the core conflict is resolved."},{"scenario":"Delivery is complete but location stream hasn't terminated — courier's post-delivery movements are exposed.","expected_behavior":"Location sharing must cease immediately upon delivery confirmation. This is an invariant regardless of which policy wins."},{"scenario":"Courier's personal safety — location data could be used to intercept or confront a courier.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the core conflict is resolved, but courier safety is a non-negotiable constraint."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Resolving this wrong in either direction has significant financial consequences. Showing exact GPS may accelerate courier attrition (recruiting and onboarding a new courier costs $3K-$5K in the logistics industry). Not showing location at all may increase customer churn and support call volume (\"where is my package?\" calls are typically 30-40% of inbound support). A middle-ground approach (e.g., zone-based tracking, ETA-only) could satisfy both sides at lower cost.","operational":"Courier retention is an existential operational concern — without drivers, there is no service. Customer satisfaction is equally critical — without customers, there is no revenue. Operations is signaling a real workforce crisis. Customer Success is signaling a real competitive positioning need. Both are operationally valid.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability and a sense of control. Couriers want dignity and privacy during work. These are both legitimate user needs. The question is whether 'exact GPS coordinates at all times' is the only way to deliver predictability, and whether 'no location shown ever' is the only way to deliver privacy. Almost certainly, neither extreme is the true requirement.","risk":"HIGH. Shipping exact GPS tracking without resolving courier consent and privacy concerns exposes the company to: (1) labor relations risk — potential regulatory action in jurisdictions with worker surveillance laws (EU GDPR, California CCPA), (2) workforce attrition risk — Operations is already reporting driver loss, (3) legal liability — if a customer uses location data to confront or harm a courier. Shipping no tracking at all risks competitive disadvantage and customer churn.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery visibility. Exact GPS is one solution but not the only one. ETA, zone-based tracking, or milestone updates may satisfy the underlying need."},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Directly affected by any location sharing. Reporting feeling surveilled. Retention is already declining. Their consent and comfort is a workforce constraint."},{"group":"Customer Success Team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Their requirement reflects customer feedback but may be over-specified (exact GPS vs. general visibility)."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Owns courier workforce. Their requirement reflects real attrition data but may be over-specified (no visibility vs. privacy-respecting visibility)."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED but must be. Worker location surveillance has legal implications in many jurisdictions. This stakeholder's absence is itself a risk."},{"group":"Executive Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a business strategy decision, not a feature decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is 40 because there is a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams, PLUS a missing stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) whose input could materially change the requirements. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this contradiction is resolved at the executive level. The spec as given is internally inconsistent and unimplementable as stated.","high_confidence_areas":["Location data must never be shared after delivery is complete — this is an invariant regardless of policy","Courier safety is a non-negotiable constraint","The underlying customer need is delivery predictability, not necessarily raw GPS","The underlying courier need is workplace dignity, not necessarily zero tracking","Legal/Compliance must be consulted before any implementation"],"low_confidence_areas":["What level of location granularity (if any) should be shared with customers","Whether courier consent is required per-delivery or as employment terms","Whether the solution is a spectrum (exact GPS → zone → ETA → milestones → nothing) or binary","What jurisdictional privacy regulations apply to this workforce"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith and representing real stakeholder concerns","There is no existing executive decision or policy that already resolves this","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker surveillance laws may apply"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires 'exact GPS coordinates at all times during delivery' as non-negotiable. Operations requires 'courier location must never be shown to customers' as non-negotiable. These are mutually exclusive requirements from equally authoritative stakeholder teams. This cannot be resolved at the feature level — it is a business strategy decision requiring executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins) — risks courier attrition and potential legal exposure","B: No location sharing at all (Operations wins) — risks customer churn and competitive disadvantage","C: Privacy-respecting middle ground — e.g., zone-based tracking ('Your courier is 3 stops away'), ETA-only, or milestone updates ('Courier has picked up your package', 'Courier is nearby'). This reframes the problem: customers get predictability, couriers get privacy","D: Opt-in model — couriers consent to location sharing as part of delivery acceptance, customers see location only when courier has opted in"],"recommendation":"Option C. Both teams have over-specified their requirements. Customer Success actually needs 'delivery predictability and transparency' — not necessarily raw GPS. Operations actually needs 'courier privacy and dignity' — not necessarily zero tracking. A zone-based or ETA-based approach (e.g., 'Courier is 5 minutes away' or 'Courier is in your area') satisfies the underlying business need of both teams without the extremes. However, I am NOT authorized to make this call — this must go to executive leadership with Legal/Compliance input. I refuse to define acceptance criteria for either extreme until this is resolved."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal/Compliance has not been consulted. Worker location surveillance is regulated in multiple jurisdictions (GDPR Article 88, CCPA, various US state laws). Any solution that shares courier location — whether exact or approximate — must be reviewed for legal compliance. This is a blocking dependency.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Engage Legal/Compliance immediately to assess regulatory constraints","Delay feature definition until legal review is complete"],"recommendation":"Engage Legal/Compliance immediately. Their input may eliminate some options entirely and simplify the executive decision."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement 'exact GPS coordinates at all times' — directly contradicted by Operations requirement and raises courier safety and legal concerns","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be defined when two authoritative teams contradict each other"},{"violation":"Cannot implement 'never show location' — directly contradicted by Customer Success requirement and ignores legitimate customer need for delivery predictability","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be defined when two authoritative teams contradict each other"},{"violation":"Acceptance criteria cannot be authored against a contradictory spec — doing so would encode one team's position as 'correct' without executive authorization","business_rule_broken":"Spec integrity — acceptance criteria must trace to agreed-upon business rules, not contested ones"}]}
